findings.title: Database credentials stored in plaintext ConfigMap
findings.description: The sigma1-infra-endpoints ConfigMap (subtask 1007) includes the PostgreSQL connection URL with the username in plaintext. While the password is omitted from the ConfigMap itself, the design instructs services to read PGPASSWORD from the sigma1-postgres-app Secret and combine it with the URL at runtime. However, the ConfigMap value 'postgresql://sigma1_user@...' still exposes the database username. More critically, several service Deployment manifests (2011, 3012, 4013) use envFrom with configMapRef, which injects all ConfigMap values as environment variables — including connection strings that may be logged by frameworks or exposed via /proc/environ if a container is compromised.
findings.recommendation: Mount connection strings as Secret volumes or projected volumes rather than environment variables. Avoid placing any credential component (including usernames) in ConfigMaps. Use CNPG-generated Secrets directly and construct connection strings in application initialization code.

findings.title: GDPR internal endpoints rely on weak defense-in-depth headers instead of mutual authentication
findings.description: All five services implement GDPR export/delete endpoints protected only by a simple X-Internal-Request or X-Internal-Key header check (subtasks 2010, 3011, 4012, 5007, 6014). While CiliumNetworkPolicies are the primary enforcement mechanism, header checks are trivially spoofable if an attacker gains access to any pod within the allowed network segment. The GDPR delete operation is destructive and irreversible — it deserves stronger authentication.
findings.recommendation: Implement mutual TLS (mTLS) between Morgan and each service for internal GDPR calls, or use a dedicated internal JWT signed with a separate key that only the GDPR orchestrator possesses. At minimum, the X-Internal-Key should be a cryptographically random token (32+ bytes) injected from a Kubernetes Secret, not a static string.

findings.title: JWT_SECRET shared across all services without key rotation strategy
findings.description: A single JWT_SECRET Kubernetes Secret (subtask 1005) is shared across all services (equipment-catalog, finance, customer-vetting, social-engine) and presumably Morgan for token generation. Compromise of any one service's environment exposes the signing key for all services. There is no JWT key rotation mechanism, key pair (asymmetric) signing, or JWKS endpoint defined. Decision point 4 in Finance acknowledges this ambiguity but it remains unresolved.
findings.recommendation: Switch to asymmetric JWT signing (RS256/ES256) with a centralized JWKS endpoint or mount public keys in consuming services. Implement key rotation by supporting multiple active verification keys. At minimum, document the JWT_SECRET rotation procedure in the ops runbooks alongside other secrets.

findings.title: Stripe webhook endpoint signature validation implementation is underspecified
findings.description: The Finance service Stripe webhook handler (subtask 4005) describes validating the Stripe-Signature header but the implementation approach is loosely specified ('stripe-rust verify_webhook or manual HMAC-SHA256 computation with reqwest'). Incorrect Stripe webhook signature validation is a common source of payment fraud vulnerabilities. The raw body must be read before any parsing/deserialization, which Axum's default body extraction may interfere with.
findings.recommendation: Mandate reading the raw request body as bytes before any JSON deserialization. Use the stripe-rust crate's built-in Webhook::construct_event() function which handles timing-safe comparison. Add explicit test cases for replay attacks (stale timestamps) and tampered payloads.

findings.title: Signal-CLI uses :latest tag with no image pinning or vulnerability scanning
findings.description: The Signal-CLI deployment (subtask 1006) uses image 'signalapp/signal-cli:latest' which is mutable and not pinned to a digest. This creates supply chain risk — the image could be updated upstream with vulnerabilities or malicious code. Additionally, Signal-CLI is deployed with a persistent volume containing sensitive cryptographic material (Signal registration keys) but there is no mention of encrypting the PVC at rest.
findings.recommendation: Pin the Signal-CLI image to a specific version tag and SHA256 digest (e.g., signalapp/signal-cli:0.13.1@sha256:abc...). Scan the image with Trivy or Snyk before deployment. Enable PVC encryption at rest via the StorageClass encryptionAtRest parameter or use an encrypted storage provider.

findings.title: No pod security standards or SecurityContext defined for any workload
findings.description: None of the Kubernetes Deployment manifests across all services (subtasks 2011, 3012, 4013, 5007, 6014, 7008) define SecurityContext settings such as runAsNonRoot, readOnlyRootFilesystem, allowPrivilegeEscalation: false, or capabilities drop. Without these, containers run as root by default and can potentially escape to the host.
findings.recommendation: Add a pod-level SecurityContext to every Deployment: runAsNonRoot: true, runAsUser: 1000, fsGroup: 1000. Add container-level SecurityContext: allowPrivilegeEscalation: false, readOnlyRootFilesystem: true (with tmpfs for writable dirs), capabilities: { drop: [ALL] }. Enforce via a PodSecurity admission controller at the namespace level (restricted profile).

findings.title: Financial data stored without encryption at rest or column-level encryption
findings.description: The Finance service (Task 4) stores sensitive financial data including invoice amounts, payment records, and Stripe IDs in plaintext in PostgreSQL. There is no mention of PostgreSQL TDE, column-level encryption for stripe_invoice_id/stripe_payment_id fields, or PII encryption for worker_name in payroll_entries.
findings.recommendation: Enable PostgreSQL Transparent Data Encryption if available in CNPG, or ensure the underlying storage class uses encrypted volumes. Apply column-level encryption using pgcrypto for stripe_payment_id, stripe_invoice_id, and worker_name fields. Document the encryption key management strategy.

findings.title: SQL injection risk in dynamic query building for catalog product search
findings.description: The equipment catalog handler (subtask 2004) uses sqlx QueryBuilder with dynamic WHERE clause construction for category_id and search parameters. While sqlx parameterized queries prevent classic SQL injection, the dynamic query building pattern using string concatenation in QueryBuilder can introduce injection risks if not carefully implemented, especially with the ILIKE operator.
findings.recommendation: Ensure all dynamic query parameters use sqlx bind parameters ($1, $2, etc.) and never string interpolation. For ILIKE patterns, sanitize the search input by escaping SQL wildcards (%, _) before wrapping in % for ILIKE. Add fuzz testing for the search parameter with malicious inputs.

findings.title: No rate limiting or authentication on GDPR deletion endpoints
findings.description: The GDPR delete endpoints across all services can delete or anonymize customer data irreversibly. While network policies restrict access, there is no rate limiting on these endpoints and the X-Internal-Key authentication is weak. A compromised Morgan pod could issue mass deletion requests.
findings.recommendation: Add rate limiting on GDPR delete endpoints (max 10 deletions per minute). Require a human-in-the-loop confirmation step before executing deletions (the skill prompt mentions this but it's not enforced at the API level). Log all GDPR deletion requests to the audit schema before execution with the requesting identity.

findings.title: Vetting service stores sensitive third-party PII in JSONB without access controls
findings.description: The customer vetting service (Task 5) stores OpenCorporates company data, LinkedIn follower counts, and Google Reviews data in JSONB columns. This third-party data may be subject to those platforms' Terms of Service regarding data storage and retention. The data is accessible to anyone with the sigma1_vetting database role.
findings.recommendation: Add data retention policies — auto-purge third-party cached data older than 90 days via a scheduled task. Encrypt the opencorporates_data JSONB column at rest using pgcrypto. Review OpenCorporates, LinkedIn, and Google API ToS for data storage compliance requirements.

findings.title: Equipment API machine-readable catalog endpoint has no authentication
findings.description: GET /api/v1/equipment-api/catalog (subtask 2007) is described as a machine-readable endpoint for AI agents but there is no indication it requires JWT authentication. This endpoint exposes the entire product catalog with specs, pricing, and availability windows — competitive business intelligence that should not be publicly accessible.
findings.recommendation: Require JWT authentication on the /api/v1/equipment-api/* endpoints. If public access is intentional for AI agents, implement API key authentication with rate limiting and consider serving a reduced dataset (no pricing) on the unauthenticated endpoint.

findings.title: Morgan PVC contains Signal cryptographic keys without encryption or backup
findings.description: The Morgan deployment (subtask 7008) uses a PVC for Signal-CLI data containing private cryptographic keys for the registered Signal number. Loss of this data means loss of the Signal identity. The PVC has no encryption at rest specified and no backup strategy. Decision point dp-10-1 acknowledges this risk but it remains unresolved.
findings.recommendation: Encrypt the PVC at rest via StorageClass encryption. Implement a CronJob that backs up the Signal-CLI data directory to R2 with encryption. Consider using a StatefulSet for Morgan to ensure PVC persistence across rescheduling.

findings.title: No audit logging for administrative actions across services
findings.description: While GDPR deletions are logged to the audit schema, there is no general audit trail for administrative actions such as: creating/updating products (admin-only endpoints in catalog), creating invoices, approving social media posts, or modifying opportunity statuses. This makes incident investigation and compliance auditing difficult.
findings.recommendation: Implement an audit logging middleware that records all write operations (POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE) to the audit schema with: timestamp, user_id (from JWT), action, resource_type, resource_id, and request body summary. This should be a shared middleware pattern across all services.

findings.title: Container base images not pinned to digests and no image scanning pipeline
findings.description: Service Dockerfiles (e.g., subtask 2011) use base images like 'rust:1.75-slim' and 'debian:bookworm-slim' without SHA256 digest pinning. The CI/CD security pipeline (subtask 10015) mentions cargo-audit and npm audit for dependency scanning but does not include container image scanning (Trivy, Snyk Container, or Grype).
findings.recommendation: Pin all base images to SHA256 digests. Add a container image scanning step to the CI/CD pipeline using Trivy: `trivy image --severity CRITICAL,HIGH --exit-code 1 ghcr.io/sigma1/<service>:$TAG`. Block deployment if CRITICAL vulnerabilities are found.

findings.title: Web chat widget CORS is set to wildcard origin
findings.description: The Morgan web chat adapter (subtask 7007) configures cors_origins: ['*'] which allows any website to embed and interact with the Morgan chat endpoint. This enables CSRF-like attacks where a malicious site could interact with Morgan on behalf of a victim.
findings.recommendation: Restrict CORS origins to the Sigma-1 domain(s): ['https://sigma1.com', 'https://www.sigma1.com', 'https://chat.sigma1.com']. The subtask notes 'tighten to Sigma-1 domain post-launch' but this should be configured from the start, with environment-specific values.

findings.title: Social media API tokens stored as Kubernetes Secrets without scoping
findings.description: Social media platform tokens (Instagram, Facebook, LinkedIn, TikTok) required by the social-engine service are not explicitly listed in the Kubernetes Secrets created in subtask 1005. These long-lived OAuth tokens for business accounts represent high-value targets. There is also no separation between read-only and write tokens.
findings.recommendation: Create a dedicated sigma1-social-tokens Secret with explicit keys for each platform's access token. Use the narrowest OAuth scopes possible. Implement token refresh logic in the social-engine service. Add these tokens to the secret rotation runbook with platform-specific refresh procedures.

findings.title: Multipart file upload endpoint lacks file type and size validation
findings.description: The social media upload endpoint POST /api/v1/social/upload (subtask 6004) accepts multipart file uploads but the implementation details do not specify file type validation (magic bytes, not just extension), maximum file size limits, or virus/malware scanning. Uploaded files are stored directly to R2.
findings.recommendation: Validate uploaded files by checking magic bytes (not just Content-Type header) to confirm JPEG/PNG format. Enforce a maximum file size (e.g., 20MB per file, 100MB total per request). Consider routing uploads through ClamAV or similar scanning before R2 storage.

findings.title: No centralized security event logging or anomaly detection
findings.description: While Prometheus metrics and Loki logging are mentioned, there is no dedicated security event logging stream. Events like failed JWT validations, rate limit triggers, GDPR deletions, admin actions, and Stripe webhook failures should be aggregated in a security-specific log stream for SIEM-like analysis.
findings.recommendation: Define a structured security event log format (JSON with event_type, severity, source_service, actor, target, timestamp). Route security events to a dedicated Loki label stream ({stream='security'}). Create Grafana dashboard panels for failed auth attempts, rate limit triggers, and GDPR operations.

findings.title: ArgoCD Application CRs use automated sync with prune without change approval
findings.description: ArgoCD Applications (subtask 10013) are configured with automated sync, selfHeal, and prune: true. While convenient, this means any commit to the main branch of the GitOps repo will automatically apply to production including resource deletions (prune). A compromised CI pipeline or accidental merge could cause destructive changes.
findings.recommendation: Require manual sync approval for production Applications or implement a two-stage promotion (auto-sync staging, manual-sync production). At minimum, disable prune: true for production and handle resource cleanup manually.

findings.title: GDPR export aggregation may expose cross-service data correlation
findings.description: The sigma1_gdpr_export tool (subtask 7004) aggregates data from all 5 services into a single JSON response keyed by service name. While this is required for GDPR subject access requests, the aggregated response creates a comprehensive profile that is more sensitive than any individual service's data. Transit security for this aggregated payload should be considered.
findings.recommendation: Ensure GDPR export responses are encrypted in transit (already covered by internal ClusterIP networking, but verify no logging middleware captures the response body). Add a TTL to the export — the aggregated response should not be cached in Valkey or any intermediate store.

strengths: CiliumNetworkPolicies provide strong zero-trust pod-to-pod isolation with per-service policies defined for all 6 services plus default-deny baseline in scale tasks
GDPR compliance is comprehensively addressed across all 5 backend services with both export and delete endpoints, plus an orchestration layer through Morgan's MCP tools
Per-schema PostgreSQL roles (sigma1_catalog, sigma1_rms, etc.) enforce database-level access isolation between services, limiting blast radius of SQL injection
Rate limiting is implemented at the application layer (Valkey sliding window in equipment-catalog) and planned at the Cloudflare edge (200 req/min per IP)
The CI/CD pipeline includes security scanning via Semgrep, cargo-audit, nancy (Go), and npm audit with merge blocking on CRITICAL/HIGH findings
Stripe webhook endpoint correctly specifies reading raw request body before deserialization for HMAC validation — a frequently missed security requirement
Secret rotation is addressed with a CronJob reminder system and documented runbook, acknowledging the trade-off vs automated rotation for v1
Health endpoints are consistently separated from business logic across all services, preventing information leakage through health check responses
summary: The Sigma-1 platform plan demonstrates strong network-level security through CiliumNetworkPolicies and comprehensive GDPR compliance design. However, there are critical gaps in secret management (shared JWT key without rotation, credentials in ConfigMaps), container security (no SecurityContext/pod security standards, unpinned images), and authentication for internal endpoints (weak header-based auth for destructive GDPR operations). The most urgent items to address before production are: migrating connection strings from ConfigMaps to Secrets (SEC-001), implementing asymmetric JWT signing (SEC-003), adding SecurityContext to all workloads (SEC-006), and strengthening GDPR endpoint authentication (SEC-002).
